note: Operation Vulindlela championed the Electricity Regulation Amendment Act as the centrepiece of energy reform.
Eskom unbundling is a flagship Operation Vulindlela reform with direct Presidential accountability.
Transnet private sector participation is an Operation Vulindlela priority under the logistics reform track.
The IRP 2024 update is a cross-departmental coordination priority for Operation Vulindlela.
Grid expansion and renewable integration are critical to ending load-shedding, the Presidency's top economic priority.
NERSA independence is an Operation Vulindlela quick win to improve regulatory credibility.
Freight rail third-party access is a core logistics reform tracked by Operation Vulindlela.
Port productivity improvement is central to the Presidency's export competitiveness agenda.
BizPortal and red tape reduction are Operation Vulindlela deliverables on SMME regulatory reform.
FATF greylisting exit was a top Presidential priority, now implemented, with ongoing monitoring.
SARS capacity expansion is backed by the Presidency as a fiscally positive governance reform.
The Presidency supports NHI in principle but recognises phased implementation depends on fiscal space and provincial capacity.
AfCFTA implementation is a Presidential priority for South Africa's continental trade leadership.
The reading crisis programme aligns with the Presidency's human capital agenda as a long-term growth enabler.
Fiscal consolidation and debt stabilisation is Treasury's core institutional mandate.
Treasury co-manages SARS capacity expansion, which yields R10-12 return per rand invested.
Treasury's PPP Unit reform is an internally driven initiative to unlock infrastructure financing.
Treasury co-led the FATF greylisting exit as a joint priority with the Presidency.
Treasury designed the Eskom debt relief conditions framework to reduce contingent liabilities.
The competitive electricity market reduces Eskom's fiscal drain and crowds in private capital.
Eskom unbundling is essential to ending the cycle of bailouts and contingent liabilities.
Treasury has consistently flagged NHI as fiscally unsustainable without credible funding mechanisms beyond existing health budgets.
Treasury supports expenditure reprioritisation but is cautious about political feasibility of redirecting grants toward employment programmes.
Treasury opposes new nuclear build due to massive upfront capital costs and long-term contingent liabilities given Eskom's fiscal history.
Treasury supports the National Transmission Company in principle but requires a clear capitalisation plan that does not expand government guarantees.
BizPortal integration is fiscally neutral and aligns with Treasury's preference for regulatory reform over spending programmes.
Treasury supports NSFAS reform only if it reduces the unsustainable funding trajectory and introduces means-testing discipline.
Transnet private sector participation reduces contingent liabilities and brings private capital to infrastructure.
Carbon tax Phase 2 generates revenue while aligning with climate commitments and CBAM readiness.
COSATU accepts Eskom unbundling only with just transition guarantees, skills retraining, and wage protections for affected workers.
COSATU demands that Transnet concessioning include labour protections, minimum wage conditions, and transition support.
COSATU conditionally supports the competitive electricity market if IPP contracts include decent work conditions and local content.
COSATU supports SMME regulatory burden reduction but opposes any weakening of labour protections under the LRA or BCEA.
COSATU supports BizPortal simplification as it does not threaten worker protections while reducing bureaucratic friction.
COSATU opposes fiscal consolidation that implies austerity cuts to public sector wages and social spending.
COSATU opposes redirecting social grants toward employment programmes, viewing grants as a constitutional safety net.
COSATU is a strong NHI advocate, viewing universal healthcare as a fundamental worker right and ANC alliance commitment.
COSATU supports EPWP reform toward skills pathways as a mechanism for labour-intensive public employment.
COSATU supports the 30% SMME procurement set-aside as a tool for broad-based job creation.
COSATU accepts freight rail third-party access only with labour protections preventing a race to the bottom on wages.
COSATU opposes PFMA and procurement flexibility reforms that could weaken accountability and facilitate outsourcing.
SMME regulatory burden reduction is a top BUSA priority as it directly improves the business environment for members.
BizPortal integration and compliance simplification are flagship BUSA advocacy items on red tape reduction.
BUSA championed the Electricity Regulation Amendment Act as critical for energy security and investment certainty.
BUSA supports Eskom unbundling as necessary for competitive energy markets and private sector participation.
BUSA advocates for Transnet private sector participation to restore logistics competitiveness.
Freight rail third-party access is a long-standing BUSA demand to break the Transnet monopoly.
Port productivity improvement is critical for BUSA members dependent on competitive export logistics.
Industrial energy self-generation reforms were a direct BUSA campaign success, enabling businesses to bypass Eskom constraints.
BUSA strongly supports NERSA independence to ensure predictable, rational energy regulation.
BUSA supports AfCFTA implementation as it opens a $3 trillion continental market for SA businesses.
AGOA retention is critical for BUSA members in automotive, textiles, and agricultural exports.
BUSA supported the FATF exit as essential for financial system credibility and correspondent banking relationships.
BUSA supports localisation in principle but opposes prescriptive designation policies that raise procurement costs without building domestic capacity.
BUSA opposes the current NHI funding model, viewing the additional payroll tax as an employment-destroying burden on formal businesses.
Critical minerals beneficiation is a core DTIC industrial policy objective for value-added manufacturing.
APDP Phase 2 is a DTIC-designed programme and its flagship sectoral intervention.
The EV White Paper is a DTIC-led initiative to manage the automotive transition and preserve manufacturing jobs.
The R-CTFL Master Plan is a DTIC sectoral partnership maintaining 100,000+ jobs in clothing and textiles.
The Steel and Metal Fabrication Master Plan is a DTIC-led intervention to protect and grow domestic metalworking.
SEZ reform is a DTIC mandate, though the department acknowledges performance has been mixed.
DTIC supports AfCFTA but insists on industrial policy safeguards to prevent premature liberalisation harming SA manufacturing.
DTIC actively works on AGOA retention given its importance for SA automotive and textile exports.
Poultry anti-dumping is a DTIC priority protecting local production and rural employment.
The Competition Commission Digital Markets Inquiry falls within DTIC's competition policy mandate.
BBBEE Equity Equivalent expansion is a DTIC transformation objective for multinational investment.
Localisation and designation policy is a core DTIC instrument for building domestic manufacturing capacity.
DTIC supports Transnet reform only if logistics liberalisation includes local content protections preventing hollowing out of domestic manufacturing.
NERSA accepts the competitive electricity market but requires adequate capacity and clear mandate boundaries in the unbundled system.
NERSA supports institutional independence in principle but is cautious about reform designs that reduce its statutory jurisdiction.
Municipal tariff rationalisation and cost-reflective pricing is core NERSA regulatory mandate.
NERSA supports the IRP update but insists its tariff methodology must be respected in any revised electricity mix.
NERSA resisted the self-generation registration framework as it bypasses NERSA's licensing jurisdiction.
NERSA supports grid integration but requires adequate regulatory capacity to oversee an expanded multi-player transmission system.
NERSA accepts Eskom unbundling but requires clear regulatory boundaries with the new Eskom Transmission entity.
Eskom accepts unbundling with a credible debt restructuring plan, adequate transition period, and ring-fenced grid investment.
Eskom cautiously accepts the competitive market but warns rapid IPP integration risks grid instability without system operator investment.
Eskom supports grid expansion but requires sufficient capitalisation of the transmission entity to manage high-renewables integration.
Eskom supports National Transmission Company capitalisation as essential for grid reliability during the transition.
Eskom supports the debt relief framework as existentially necessary for financial viability during restructuring.
Eskom opposed self-generation liberalisation as it erodes its customer base and revenue without compensating grid cost allocation.
Eskom supports the IRP update but advocates for a generation mix that preserves baseload capacity during the transition.
Eskom supports cost-reflective municipal tariffs as non-cost-reflective tariffs create revenue shortfalls that undermine grid investment.
Eskom is neutral on new nuclear build, recognising baseload value but focused on managing the current fleet and unbundling.
Koeberg life extension is an Eskom operational priority maintaining 1,860 MW of baseload capacity.
Transnet accepts private sector participation only with phased implementation, network obligation protections, and transition support.
Transnet resists third-party rail access, fearing cherry-picking of profitable corridors while retaining uneconomic routes and legacy debt.
Transnet supports port productivity improvement but insists on government capex support for the maintenance backlog before private participation.
Transnet opposes an independent Transport Economic Regulator that would impose external oversight on its pricing and access decisions.
Transnet is neutral on maritime industry development, which falls partly outside its core port operations mandate.
Transnet supports AfCFTA logistics requirements but needs capital investment to restore export corridor capacity first.
NUM opposes Eskom unbundling without comprehensive just transition guarantees for energy workers, including wage parity and retraining.
NUM opposes the competitive electricity market, viewing IPP employment as inferior to Eskom jobs in wages, security, and benefits.
NUM conditionally supports renewable grid integration if coal community economic diversification plans are co-developed with measurable milestones.
NUM supports the IRP update only if the coal phase-down timeline matches just transition fund disbursement to affected communities.
NUM views industrial self-generation as undermining Eskom's revenue base and threatening energy worker employment.
NUM supports minerals beneficiation if it creates downstream manufacturing jobs compensating for potential mining employment decline.
NUM opposes Transnet privatisation without equivalent wage and job security guarantees for affected workers.
NUM conditionally supports green hydrogen if it includes job creation commitments and skills retraining for displaced fossil fuel workers.
NUM supports energy transition manufacturing skills training as a concrete pathway for workers displaced by the coal transition.
NUM conditionally accepts Eskom debt relief conditions but opposes restructuring terms that result in involuntary retrenchments.
The Digital Markets Inquiry is a Competition Commission-led initiative under its statutory mandate.
Digital Platforms Competition Framework is a direct extension of the Commission's market inquiry findings.
The Commission supports Transnet reform only if concessioning creates competitive markets rather than transferring monopoly to private hands.
Freight rail third-party access directly addresses market concentration that the Commission has identified as harmful.
Port productivity improvement through competitive structures aligns with Commission findings on logistics market concentration.
A competitive electricity market directly addresses the generation monopoly the Commission views as economically damaging.
SMME regulatory burden reduction removes barriers to entry that entrench incumbent market power.
Copyright reform and fair use provisions align with the Commission's digital competition objectives.
SARB supported the FATF exit as essential for financial system integrity and correspondent banking relationships.
SARB supports AML/CFT implementation monitoring as it strengthens the financial system's compliance framework.
SARB strongly supports fiscal consolidation as necessary to prevent fiscal dominance undermining monetary policy effectiveness.
SARB supported the two-pot pension system design within its financial stability oversight framework.
Microinsurance reform falls within SARB's Twin Peaks prudential oversight mandate for financial inclusion.
SARB supports cooperative bank development only within adequate prudential oversight frameworks to prevent systemic risk.
SARB is cautious about BRICS+ alternative payment systems that could create unregulated channels outside NPS oversight.
Carbon tax is a fiscal policy matter outside SARB's mandate, though its macroeconomic effects are monitored.
SARB monitors NHI's fiscal implications for debt sustainability but takes no direct policy stance on healthcare reform.
SARB supports Eskom debt relief conditions as they reduce contingent liabilities threatening sovereign credit and financial stability.
BUSA supports the IRP 2024 update for regulatory certainty on the generation mix underpinning energy investment decisions.
Grid expansion is essential for BUSA members investing in self-generation who need reliable transmission access.
BUSA supports TVET quality reform as businesses face severe artisan shortages constraining growth and investment.
BUSA supports fiscal consolidation as it reduces borrowing costs and improves the sovereign credit rating for business investment.
BUSA supports SEZ reform but demands streamlined regulatory approval and functioning infrastructure before new designations.
National Transmission Company capitalisation is an Operation Vulindlela tracked commitment for grid stability.
Operationalising the Transport Economic Regulator is a Presidency priority for logistics reform accountability.
TVET artisan pipeline reform is a Presidential priority for addressing structural unemployment through skills development.
BUSA supports the SMME Ombud as it provides formal dispute resolution protecting businesses from regulatory overreach.
Eskom recognises baseload value of new nuclear but its institutional focus is on completing unbundling and debt restructuring first.
NUM supports new nuclear build as it creates long-term high-quality energy sector employment comparable to coal generation.
COSATU supports nuclear new build as it creates unionisable, well-paid energy jobs unlike IPP contract work.
BUSA opposes nuclear new build due to massive cost overrun risk and the availability of cheaper renewable alternatives.
Treasury opposes new nuclear given the R200bn+ estimated cost, long construction timelines, and fiscal space constraints.
Green hydrogen is a Presidential priority for SA's energy transition and industrial competitiveness strategy.
DTIC views green hydrogen as a strategic industrial opportunity leveraging SA's renewable energy and PGM endowments.
Treasury supports green hydrogen commercialisation if structured through blended finance, not large unfunded government commitments.
BUSA supports the managed EV transition to preserve SA's automotive manufacturing export base.
NUM conditionally supports the EV transition if retraining programmes and employment guarantees are provided for ICE manufacturing workers.
COSATU supports the EV White Paper only with just transition provisions for workers in affected automotive supply chains.
AGOA retention is a Presidential diplomatic priority given its importance for manufacturing employment.
DTIC is actively engaged in AGOA negotiations to protect export-dependent manufacturing sectors.
The SMME Ombud operationalisation is an Operation Vulindlela tracked reform for business environment improvement.
BUSA supports the 30% SMME procurement set-aside in principle but demands implementation that does not compromise procurement efficiency.
Treasury supports SMME procurement access but insists on value-for-money safeguards and payment timeline enforcement.
Treasury supports SEFA mandate refocus only if recapitalisation is tied to measurable performance outcomes and reduced NPL ratios.
COSATU supports informal economy integration as it extends labour protections and social security to vulnerable workers.
BUSA is neutral on informal economy integration, recognising its importance but focused on formal sector reform priorities.
Urban land release for affordable housing is a Presidential priority for addressing spatial inequality and housing backlog.
Treasury supports urban land release if structured to generate municipal revenue rather than requiring additional fiscal transfers.
Treasury supports the reading crisis programme as a high-return human capital investment with minimal fiscal cost relative to impact.
COSATU supports the literacy programme as foundational to breaking the cycle of unemployment and inequality.
DTIC supports TVET reform as industrial master plans depend on an adequate supply of skilled artisans.
COSATU supports TVET quality reform as it creates decent employment pathways for working-class youth.
NUM supports artisan pipeline expansion as it creates skilled employment in mining, energy, and construction sectors.
Treasury supports SOE governance reform including PFMA and procurement flexibility to enable efficient restructuring.
SOE policy reform is an Operation Vulindlela priority to remove legislative obstacles to restructuring.
BUSA supports SOE governance reform as necessary for credible concessioning and private sector participation.
NUM opposes procurement flexibility reforms that could enable outsourcing and weaken SOE worker protections.
DTIC supports carbon tax if revenue is recycled into industrial competitiveness measures and CBAM readiness for SA exporters.
BUSA conditionally supports carbon tax Phase 2 if the tax rate trajectory is predictable and revenue supports green industrial transition.
NUM opposes accelerated carbon taxation as it threatens coal-dependent employment without adequate transition funding.
Critical minerals beneficiation is a Presidential strategic priority given the global energy transition supply chain opportunity.
BUSA supports beneficiation if commercially viable and supported by energy and logistics reform, not imposed through export levies.
Treasury supports the beneficiation strategy if it attracts private investment rather than requiring large public subsidies.
Treasury supports SETA reform to improve skills levy efficiency, recovering billions in under-utilised training funds.
BUSA strongly supports SETA reform, citing chronic levy-grant inefficiency that fails to deliver industry-relevant training.
COSATU supports SETA reform only if worker representatives retain governance seats and training addresses worker advancement.
SARB supports SARS capacity expansion as stronger revenue collection supports fiscal sustainability and monetary policy credibility.
National Lotteries Commission governance reform aligns with the Presidency's anti-corruption and consequence management agenda.
Treasury supports NSFAS fraud prevention as it recovers misallocated funds and improves expenditure efficiency.
COSATU supports expanded NSFAS funding as essential for working-class access to post-school education.
COSATU supports absorbing unemployed healthcare workers into the public health system as both a jobs and service delivery intervention.
Treasury supports healthcare worker absorption only within the existing compensation of employees budget ceiling.
TB elimination is a Presidential health priority given its direct impact on workforce productivity and health expenditure.
COSATU conditionally supports taxi formalisation only with genuine consultation and transition support for taxi operators and workers.
BUSA supports taxi formalisation as it improves urban mobility for workers and reduces transport costs for businesses.
Treasury supports localisation only where cost premiums are transparent and the designation process does not create procurement bottlenecks.
The Commission supports localisation but warns designation policies can shield inefficient incumbents from competitive pressure.
BUSA is split on poultry anti-dumping; importers and retailers oppose duties while producers support them.
The Commission supports anti-dumping measures only where genuine predatory pricing is demonstrated, not as general protection.
COSATU strongly supports PRASA rail recovery as affordable commuter transport directly benefits low-income workers.
PRASA recovery is a Presidential infrastructure priority for urban mobility and spatial inequality reduction.
COSATU supports integrated public transport as it reduces transport costs for workers commuting from townships.
Treasury supports public transport scale-up only with sustainable operational funding models, not open-ended subsidies.
SEZ reform is core DTIC mandate; the department is developing improved governance and incentive frameworks.
Treasury supports SEZ reform only if tax incentive costs are justified by measurable investment and employment outcomes.
Treasury supports PEPFAR funding transition planning as PEPFAR withdrawal requires domestic fiscal absorption of HIV programme costs.
PEPFAR funding transition is a Presidential diplomatic and public health priority given US funding uncertainty.
ECD migration to the Department of Basic Education is a structural reform the Presidency championed.
Treasury supports ECD function shift but insists on credible costing for the additional DBE mandate and phased implementation.
NUM supports the Just Energy Transition SMME Finance Facility as it directly funds alternative livelihoods for coal communities.
Treasury supports the JET SMME facility only if funded through JETP mechanisms rather than additional fiscal commitments.
NUM supports the derelict mines rehabilitation fund as it creates jobs in mine closure and addresses community health risks.
Treasury supports mine rehabilitation but insists the fund be structured so mining companies bear costs rather than the fiscus.
BUSA supports the Transport Economic Regulator to ensure transparent, cost-reflective pricing for freight and port services.
The Commission supports economic regulation of transport as it addresses monopoly pricing in ports and rail.
Treasury supports cost-reflective municipal tariffs as non-cost-reflective pricing creates hidden fiscal liabilities.
COSATU opposes cost-reflective tariffs without stronger cross-subsidies, fearing electricity price increases for low-income households.
Municipal fiscal powers reform is a Treasury priority for improving local government revenue and infrastructure investment capacity.
Equitable share formula review is an ongoing Treasury mandate to improve intergovernmental fiscal transfers.
Health Market Inquiry reforms are binding Commission recommendations on private healthcare market structure.
BUSA's healthcare members conditionally accept Health Market Inquiry reforms if implementation preserves investment incentives.
Fuel price partial deregulation could reduce administered price distortions that Treasury views as economically inefficient.
COSATU opposes fuel price deregulation, fearing price increases in rural areas and for low-income commuters.
BUSA supports fuel price deregulation as it removes inefficient administered pricing and enables market competition.
Integrated Energy Plan gas diversification falls within NERSA's gas pipeline regulatory mandate.
DTIC supports energy diversification through gas as it provides reliable industrial energy reducing manufacturing costs.
LNG import infrastructure falls within NERSA's gas regulatory jurisdiction and expands its mandate.
DTIC supports advanced manufacturing skills for the energy transition as it builds the workforce for EV and green hydrogen sectors.
BUSA supports energy transition manufacturing skills as industry faces severe shortages in solar, wind, and EV technicians.
Treasury supports RAF structural reform as the fund's unfunded liability represents a major contingent fiscal risk.
COSATU conditionally supports RAF reform only if accident victims retain access to adequate compensation without benefit cuts.
Treasury supports ending ongoing state support for the SAA successor entity to eliminate a persistent contingent liability.
COSATU accepts SAA commercial viability but opposes full privatisation without worker equity participation.
Treasury supports Denel strategic equity partnership to end decades of bailouts and contingent liability.
NUM conditionally supports Denel restructuring with employment transition plans for affected workers.
Treasury supports school nutrition procurement reform as it recovers funds lost to corruption and improves spending efficiency.
BUSA supports STEM teacher development as STEM skill shortages constrain industry growth across multiple sectors.
Treasury supports STEM teacher programmes only with credible retention mechanisms preventing costly training losses to private sector.
Green hydrogen R&D aligns with the Presidency's hydrogen economy strategy leveraging SA's renewable and PGM endowments.
DTIC supports hydrogen R&D as it builds the industrial base for SA's green hydrogen export ambitions.
COSATU supports the R-CTFL Master Plan as it protects 100,000+ jobs in a labour-intensive manufacturing sector.
BUSA conditionally supports R-CTFL if the master plan includes realistic competitiveness targets alongside protection measures.
COSATU supports the Steel Master Plan as it protects metalworking jobs and domestic manufacturing capability.
NUM supports the Steel Master Plan as steel fabrication provides downstream employment for mining sector workers.
BUSA supports APDP Phase 2 as automotive is SA's largest manufacturing export sector with 110,000+ direct jobs.
COSATU supports APDP enhancement as it protects well-paid manufacturing jobs in the automotive sector.
Treasury supports APDP but requires demonstrated ROI from incentives given the R2.5bn+ annual fiscal cost.
Koeberg life extension is supported by the Presidency as critical baseload capacity during the energy transition.
NERSA supports Koeberg life extension through its licensing framework to maintain baseload generation capacity.
Treasury supports the Tobacco Products Control Act as it protects sin tax revenue from illicit trade erosion.
COSATU supports the BELA Act as it advances educational equity and extends compulsory schooling.
COSATU supports primary healthcare strengthening as it improves healthcare access for workers and their families.
Treasury supports PHC platform strengthening within existing health budgets through efficiency gains rather than additional allocations.
Treasury supports provincial health department turnarounds as dysfunctional provinces waste allocated health budgets.
Provincial health turnarounds align with the Presidency's state capacity building agenda.
Treasury supports PMTE property portfolio rationalisation as it generates revenue from under-utilised government assets.
Treasury supports PPP regulatory reform for social infrastructure as it leverages private capital for public service delivery.
COSATU opposes PPPs for social infrastructure, viewing them as privatisation of public services that raises costs for communities.
BRICS+ trade facilitation aligns with the Presidency's multi-lateral foreign policy and South-South economic cooperation.
DTIC supports BRICS+ trade facilitation as it diversifies SA's export markets beyond Western economies.
DTIC supports upstream petroleum development only with adequate local content and beneficiation requirements.
Treasury supports the Petroleum Resources Development Bill as domestic oil and gas exploration could reduce import dependency and generate royalties.
Eskom supports solar water heaters as they reduce peak demand pressure on the national grid.
Treasury supports solar water heater rollout only through targeted subsidies with clear cost-benefit analysis, not open-ended programmes.
Anti-dumping and import surveillance modernisation is a core DTIC trade remedy function.
Treasury supports professional project management mandates as poor execution wastes billions in infrastructure budgets annually.
Infrastructure delivery management improvement is an Operation Vulindlela tracked commitment.
COSATU supports mental health services expansion as workplace mental health directly affects worker productivity and wellbeing.
COSATU supports mother-tongue multilingual education as it improves learning outcomes for working-class children.
DTIC supports cannabis industry rationalisation as it creates SMME opportunities in a new agricultural value chain.
Treasury supports cannabis licensing rationalisation as a revenue-generating regulatory reform at minimal fiscal cost.
Treasury supports the GEPF infrastructure mandate only with robust fiduciary safeguards protecting pension fund members' retirement savings.
SARB supports GEPF infrastructure investment only within prudential frameworks ensuring pension fund solvency and fiduciary duty.
COSATU supports directing GEPF funds to infrastructure only with ironclad guarantees protecting public sector workers' pension returns.
Self-generation reform was a flagship Operation Vulindlela achievement, removing the 100MW licensing threshold.
